macro_segments.title: Introduction and Opening Mishnah: Multiple Prohibitions and Single Warning

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context for Nazir 42b
Mishnah: Drinking wine, cutting hair, and becoming tamei—single versus multiple lashes based on warnings
Discussion of the role of hatra'ah (warning) in defining separate transgressions

macro_segments.title: Gemara Debate: Tumah v'Tumah—Whether Becoming Tamei Again Violates the Prohibition

micro_segments.title: Conceptual difference between tumah and other prohibitions: process vs. discrete acts
Rava's position: Once tamei, no new violation for touching another corpse
Rav Yosef's position: Separate touching of dead bodies counts as separate transgressions
Braita about a kohen with corpse on shoulder touching another body
Rav Yosef's response: Mishnah supports his view over the Braita

macro_segments.title: Resolving the Contradiction: Chiburim (Connected Contact) vs. Separation

micro_segments.title: Key distinction: whether one is actively touching (b'chiburim) or has removed hand (sh'lo b'chiburim)
Higher level of tumah while actively touching—daisy-chaining and avi avot hatumah
Tumah b'chiburim as derabbanan: exceptions for pesach and nazir via limiting to three links
Gemara's resolution: chiburim by adam b'meis is d'oraita; application limits for pesach and nazir

macro_segments.title: Entry into House (Bia) vs. Tumah: Two Separate Prohibitions at Simultaneous Entry

micro_segments.title: Distinction between lo yitama (state) and lo yavo (act of entering)
Question: If already tamei while in house, why do you violate lo yavo separately?
Tosafot's reading: Two prohibitions when entering; only one when touching another body while holding first
Rav Papa's case: Entering a box in cemetery, friend removes roof—simultaneous bia and tumah
Mar bar Rav Ashi: Person dying (goses) in room—whether bia and tumah occur simultaneously

macro_segments.title: Goses (Dying Person): When Does Violation Occur—At Death or Before?

micro_segments.title: Braita: Kohen may remain with dying relative until actual death; Nazir has stricter derasha
Debate between mei'hechalo (until desecration) and b'mosam (at death) interpretations
Whether goses is included: lehachelo suggests yes; b'mosam suggests only after death
Braita: Person does not cause tumah until soul departs—even if dying or mutilated
Distinction: For tumah status, must wait for death; for chillul (desecration), may include goses
Shulchan Aruch ruling: Goses does not transmit tumah; nevertheless assur (forbidden) to enter—ambiguous level

macro_segments.title: Practical Halakhic Consequences: Burying Relatives and Kohen Restrictions

micro_segments.title: Tosafot: Kohen cannot become tamei to other relatives same day even if already tamei to one
Problem of kohen exiting cemetery: Walking out risks becoming tamei to other graves
Tosafot's solution: Burial at edge of cemetery to minimize exposure; practice of no trees

macro_segments.title: Ra'avad's Radical Reinterpretation: Reading Rav Yosef vs. Rava Differently

micro_segments.title: Ra'avad's claim: The whole sugya on actively touching was Rav Yosef's strict position, not lenient
Ra'avad: Rava's lenient position—no lashes even after removing hand and touching again
Ra'avad conclusion: Hilkhata k'Rava; kohanim nowadays exempt even days later
Question of muttar vs. patur; application to kohanim as doctors and pikuach nefesh

topical_tags.term: Tumah v'tumah—multiple acts of becoming tamei
Chiburim (connected contact) and level of tumah
Lo yitama (do not become tamei—state) vs. lo yavo (do not enter—act)
Goses (dying person) and when violation occurs
Pesach and nazir exceptions to full seven-day tumah
Braita vs. Mishnah contradiction on tumah v'tumah
Ra'avad's reinterpretation of Rav Yosef and Rava
Kohen as doctor and pikuach nefesh
